title: Osteomyelitis
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Internal Medicine, Orthopedics, Infectious Disease
objectives: Classify osteomyelitis by mechanism and chronicity
Apply diagnostic approach including imaging and culture
Understand role of MRI vs other imaging modalities
Select appropriate antibiotic regimens and duration

vignette.text:
A 62-year-old man with diabetes presents with 3 weeks of progressively worsening low back pain radiating to both legs. He reports fevers and chills for the past week. Pain is worse at night and not relieved by rest.

Past Medical History: Type 2 diabetes (A1c 9.2%), hypertension, recent UTI treated 6 weeks ago

Medications: Metformin, lisinopril

Allergies: NKDA

physicalExam.back: Midline lumbar tenderness L3-L4, paraspinal muscle spasm
physicalExam.neuro: 5/5 strength bilateral lower extremities, intact sensation, normal reflexes

questions.stem: This patient has back pain, fever, and elevated inflammatory markers.
questions.question: How is osteomyelitis classified?

options.text: Chronic is defined as >2 weeks duration
All osteomyelitis is from direct inoculation
Hematogenous spread only occurs in children

OSTEOMYELITIS CLASSIFICATION: BY MECHANISM: (1) HEMATOGENOUS: Bloodstream spread; Adults: vertebral most common; Children: long bone metaphysis; Single organism typical; (2) CONTIGUOUS: Direct spread from adjacent infection; Often polymicrobial; (3) DIRECT INOCULATION: Trauma, surgery; BY DURATION: ACUTE (<2 weeks), SUBACUTE (2wk-3mo), CHRONIC (>3mo or sequestrum); BY LOCATION: Vertebral, Long bone, Diabetic foot, Prosthetic joint; This patient: HEMATOGENOUS vertebral osteomyelitis (likely from prior UTI seeding)

questions.explanation: Osteomyelitis Classification: BY MECHANISM: HEMATOGENOUS: Bloodstream spread to bone. Adults: Vertebral column most common. Children: Long bone metaphysis. Single organism typical. CONTIGUOUS: From adjacent soft tissue infection. Surgical sites, pressure ulcers, diabetic foot. Often polymicrobial. DIRECT INOCULATION: Open fractures, surgery, trauma. BY DURATION: ACUTE: <2 weeks symptoms. SUBACUTE: 2 weeks to 3 months. CHRONIC: >3 months OR sequestrum formation. This patient has hematogenous vertebral osteomyelitis, likely seeded from recent UTI bacteremia.

sources.text: StatPearls: Osteomyelitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What are the common pathogens causing osteomyelitis?

options.text: Polymicrobial only in trauma

OSTEOMYELITIS PATHOGENS: HEMATOGENOUS/VERTEBRAL: S. aureus: Most common ALL ages (50-70%); MRSA increasingly common; Gram-negatives (E. coli, Pseudomonas) - especially GU source; Streptococci; CONTIGUOUS/DIABETIC FOOT: Polymicrobial common; S. aureus, Streptococci, Enterococci; Gram-negatives, Anaerobes; SPECIAL POPULATIONS: Sickle cell: Salmonella (classic) + S. aureus; IVDU: S. aureus, Pseudomonas, Candida; Post-op: S. aureus, CoNS; This patient: S. aureus most likely, gram-negative possible given UTI source
Gram-negatives never cause vertebral osteomyelitis
Streptococcus is most common overall

questions.explanation: Osteomyelitis Microbiology: S. aureus is the most common cause in ALL age groups (50-70%). MRSA is increasingly prevalent. Gram-negatives (E. coli, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas) are more common with GU source. Diabetic foot and contiguous infections are often polymicrobial. Special associations: Sickle cell with Salmonella, IVDU with Pseudomonas/Candida.

questions.stem: You need to confirm the diagnosis.
questions.question: What is the diagnostic approach to vertebral osteomyelitis?

options.text: Blood cultures are rarely positive
Biopsy is never needed
Plain X-rays are the gold standard

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH (IDSA 2015): CLINICAL: Back pain unresponsive to conservative measures; Elevated inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP); Fever (present in ~50%); IMAGING: Plain X-rays: NOT sensitive for early disease; MRI: Gold standard (sensitivity 97%, specificity 93%); T1 hypointense, T2/STIR hyperintense; Disc involvement, paraspinal extension; CT: Alternative if MRI contraindicated; MICROBIOLOGY: Blood cultures BEFORE antibiotics (positive 30-70%); Image-guided biopsy if blood cultures negative; Hold antibiotics until cultures obtained (if stable); LABS: ESR and CRP elevated in >90%; CRP more responsive to treatment

questions.explanation: Diagnostic Approach to Vertebral Osteomyelitis (IDSA 2015): CLINICAL: Back pain unresponsive to conservative measures with elevated inflammatory markers. Fever present in about 50%. IMAGING: MRI is GOLD STANDARD with 97% sensitivity and 93% specificity. Shows T1 hypointensity, T2/STIR hyperintensity, disc involvement. Plain X-rays are NOT sensitive early. MICROBIOLOGY: Blood cultures positive in 30-70%. Image-guided biopsy if blood cultures negative. CRITICAL: Hold antibiotics until cultures obtained if patient is hemodynamically stable.

sources.text: StatPearls: Osteomyelitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Blood cultures are negative after 48 hours.
questions.question: When is bone biopsy indicated?

options.text: Biopsy required in all cases

BONE BIOPSY INDICATIONS (IDSA 2015): Blood cultures NEGATIVE with high clinical suspicion; Atypical presentation; Immunocompromised patient (broader differential); Failed empiric therapy; Need to identify organism for directed therapy; BIOPSY TECHNIQUES: Image-guided percutaneous (CT or fluoroscopy): First-line; Yield 30-90% depending on technique; Open biopsy: If percutaneous non-diagnostic; Higher yield but more invasive; OPTIMIZING YIELD: Hold antibiotics 48-72h before biopsy if possible; Send for culture AND histopathology; Include AFB and fungal cultures if risk factors; WHEN BIOPSY NOT NEEDED: Positive blood cultures with consistent organism + compatible MRI
Continue antibiotics through biopsy
Histopathology is not needed

questions.explanation: Bone Biopsy in Vertebral Osteomyelitis: INDICATIONS: Negative blood cultures with high clinical suspicion. Atypical presentation. Immunocompromised (broader differential). Failed empiric therapy. TECHNIQUE: Image-guided percutaneous biopsy is first-line (CT or fluoroscopy guided). Open biopsy if percutaneous non-diagnostic. OPTIMIZING YIELD: Hold antibiotics 48-72 hours before biopsy if stable. Send for culture AND histopathology. Include AFB and fungal cultures if indicated. NOT NEEDED if positive blood cultures with compatible MRI findings.

sources.text: StatPearls: Osteomyelitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What is the antibiotic treatment for vertebral osteomyelitis?

options.text: VERTEBRAL OSTEOMYELITIS TREATMENT: MSSA: Nafcillin/Oxacillin 2g IV q4h or Cefazolin 2g IV q8h; MRSA: Vancomycin 15-20mg/kg IV q8-12h (trough 15-20); Streptococci: Penicillin G or Ceftriaxone; Gram-negatives: Based on susceptibilities (often ceftriaxone or fluoroquinolone); DURATION: 6 WEEKS is standard recommendation; Count from first negative blood culture or surgery; IV-TO-ORAL TRANSITION: Emerging data supports early oral switch; After clinical improvement and CRP decrease; Requires highly bioavailable oral agent; ADJUNCTIVE: Glycemic control in diabetics; Pain management; Physical therapy after acute phase
Vancomycin is first-line for MSSA
2 weeks of antibiotics is sufficient
Oral therapy cannot be used

questions.explanation: Antibiotic Treatment for Vertebral Osteomyelitis: PATHOGEN-DIRECTED THERAPY: MSSA: Nafcillin/Oxacillin 2g IV q4h OR Cefazolin 2g IV q8h. MRSA: Vancomycin (trough 15-20) or Daptomycin. Streptococci: Penicillin G or Ceftriaxone. Gram-negatives: Based on susceptibilities. DURATION: Standard is 6 WEEKS. Count from first negative culture or surgery. IV-TO-ORAL: Emerging evidence supports early transition to oral therapy after clinical improvement and CRP decrease, using highly bioavailable agents.

sources.text: StatPearls: Osteomyelitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: The patient asks about oral antibiotics.
questions.question: When can oral antibiotics be used for osteomyelitis?

options.text: All osteomyelitis requires full IV course
Oral antibiotics have poor bone penetration
Switch to oral only after 4 weeks IV

ORAL ANTIBIOTIC OPTIONS: HIGH BIOAVAILABILITY AGENTS: Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin, moxifloxacin); TMP-SMX; Linezolid; Doxycycline/Minocycline; Metronidazole (anaerobes); Clindamycin; TRANSITION CRITERIA: Clinical improvement (afebrile, pain improving); Inflammatory markers decreasing (especially CRP); Organism identified and susceptible to oral agent; Able to tolerate oral intake; Adherent patient; EVIDENCE: OVIVA trial: Oral switch at 1 week non-inferior to IV; Applicable to osteomyelitis (vertebral and non-vertebral); NOT SUITABLE FOR ORAL: Inability to absorb; Non-adherent patient; No appropriate oral agent available; Endocarditis or other deep infection

questions.explanation: Oral Antibiotics in Osteomyelitis: HIGH BIOAVAILABILITY AGENTS with good bone penetration: Fluoroquinolones, TMP-SMX, Linezolid, Doxycycline/Minocycline, Clindamycin. TRANSITION CRITERIA: Clinical improvement (afebrile, pain improving). CRP decreasing. Susceptible organism identified. Can tolerate oral. Adherent patient. OVIVA TRIAL evidence supports early oral switch (1 week) as non-inferior to prolonged IV therapy for bone and joint infections.

questions.question: What are the surgical indications in vertebral osteomyelitis?

options.text: SURGICAL INDICATIONS (IDSA 2015): NEUROLOGIC: Progressive neurologic deficits (URGENT); Spinal cord compression; Cauda equina syndrome; STRUCTURAL: Spinal instability; Progressive deformity; INFECTION CONTROL: Persistent bacteremia despite appropriate antibiotics; Epidural abscess requiring drainage; Large paravertebral abscess; Failed medical therapy; IMPORTANT: Worsening MRI at 4-6 weeks ALONE is NOT indication for surgery if clinical improvement; NOT ALWAYS NEEDED: Most vertebral osteomyelitis treated medically; Surgery in ~30-40% of cases; Epidural abscess management controversial - small abscesses may be treated medically
Epidural abscess always requires drainage
All vertebral osteomyelitis requires surgery
Worsening MRI always requires surgery

questions.explanation: Surgical Indications in Vertebral Osteomyelitis (IDSA 2015): NEUROLOGIC INDICATIONS (Urgent): Progressive neurologic deficits, Spinal cord compression, Cauda equina syndrome. STRUCTURAL: Spinal instability, Progressive deformity. INFECTION CONTROL: Persistent bacteremia despite appropriate therapy, Epidural abscess requiring drainage, Failed medical therapy. IMPORTANT: Worsening imaging at 4-6 weeks is NOT an indication if clinical symptoms and inflammatory markers are improving. Most cases (60-70%) are managed medically.

questions.stem: A different patient presents with diabetic foot infection.
questions.question: How does diabetic foot osteomyelitis differ?

options.text: DFO is always monomicrobial
Vascular status is not important

DIABETIC FOOT OSTEOMYELITIS (DFO): DIAGNOSIS: Probe-to-bone test: If positive, high specificity for osteomyelitis; MRI: Gold standard imaging; Plain X-ray: May show bone destruction but less sensitive; MICROBIOLOGY: Often POLYMICROBIAL; S. aureus most common single organism; Streptococci, Enterococci; Gram-negatives (Pseudomonas, Enterobacteriaceae); Anaerobes; TREATMENT: Antibiotics PLUS wound care PLUS offloading; Duration: 4-6 weeks if no resection, shorter if bone resected; Surgery: Debridement or amputation may be needed; Vascular assessment essential (revascularization if needed); PROGNOSIS: High recurrence rate; Limb-threatening if not addressed
Probe-to-bone test has low specificity

questions.explanation: Diabetic Foot Osteomyelitis: DIAGNOSIS: Probe-to-bone test is highly specific if positive. MRI is gold standard. MICROBIOLOGY: Often polymicrobial with S. aureus most common, plus streptococci, gram-negatives, and anaerobes. TREATMENT: Requires multimodal approach - antibiotics, wound care, offloading, and vascular assessment. Duration depends on surgical management - shorter if infected bone resected. High recurrence rates require vigilant follow-up.

questions.question: How should treatment response be monitored?

options.text: ESR is more responsive than CRP
MRI should normalize within 2 weeks
Repeat MRI weekly to monitor response

MONITORING TREATMENT RESPONSE: CLINICAL: Pain improvement; Resolution of fever; Functional improvement; INFLAMMATORY MARKERS: CRP: More responsive, decreases within days; ESR: Slower to normalize (weeks); Check weekly initially; Failure to decrease suggests treatment failure; IMAGING: MRI may WORSEN at 4-6 weeks despite clinical improvement - this is expected!; Do NOT repeat MRI routinely unless clinical concern; MRI normalization takes months; BLOOD CULTURES: Repeat if initially positive; Should clear within 48-72 hours; Persistent bacteremia = treatment failure; END OF THERAPY: Clinical resolution; Normalized or significantly improved CRP; No need for normalized MRI

questions.explanation: Monitoring Osteomyelitis Treatment: CLINICAL: Track pain, fever, and function. INFLAMMATORY MARKERS: CRP is more responsive (decreases in days). ESR is slower (weeks). Check weekly initially. IMAGING CAVEAT: MRI may WORSEN at 4-6 weeks despite clinical improvement - this is expected and NOT an indication for surgery if clinical improvement. MRI normalization takes months. BLOOD CULTURES: Should clear within 48-72 hours. Persistent bacteremia indicates treatment failure.

questions.stem: MRI confirms L3-L4 osteomyelitis/discitis with small epidural phlegmon. No cord compression.
questions.question: What is the complete management approach?

options.text: Complete approach: (1) DIAGNOSIS CONFIRMED: MRI + clinical + elevated markers = vertebral osteomyelitis; (2) OBTAIN CULTURES: Blood cultures (positive 30-70%); If negative → image-guided biopsy (hold antibiotics 48-72h if stable); (3) EMPIRIC THERAPY: Cover S. aureus + gram-negatives (given UTI history); Vancomycin + Ceftriaxone until cultures; Narrow based on results; (4) NO URGENT SURGERY: No neurologic deficits; Small phlegmon, no large abscess; Medical management appropriate; (5) DURATION: 6 weeks IV (or oral switch after improvement); (6) MONITORING: Weekly CRP; Clinical assessment; Repeat cultures if positive; (7) DIABETES CONTROL: Optimize glycemic management; (8) DO NOT repeat MRI at 4-6 weeks unless clinical worsening
Start antibiotics before obtaining cultures
Urgent surgery for epidural phlegmon
2 weeks of antibiotics is sufficient

questions.explanation: Complete Management Approach: STEP 1 - CONFIRM DIAGNOSIS: MRI findings + clinical presentation + elevated inflammatory markers. STEP 2 - OBTAIN CULTURES BEFORE ANTIBIOTICS: Blood cultures first. If negative, image-guided biopsy (hold antibiotics 48-72h if hemodynamically stable). STEP 3 - EMPIRIC THERAPY: Cover S. aureus and gram-negatives given UTI history. Vancomycin + Ceftriaxone is reasonable empiric choice. Narrow once cultures return. STEP 4 - SURGICAL ASSESSMENT: No urgent surgery needed - no neurologic deficits, small phlegmon without abscess. STEP 5 - DURATION: 6 weeks (can transition to oral after clinical improvement and CRP decrease). STEP 6 - MONITORING: Weekly CRP, clinical assessment. Do NOT routine repeat MRI at 4-6 weeks - expected to look worse. STEP 7 - OPTIMIZE DIABETES: Critical for healing.

sources.text: StatPearls: Osteomyelitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

references.title: IDSA Guideline for Osteomyelitis Diagnosis & Treatment

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected osteomyelitis?

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in osteomyelitis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

options.text: Start empiric treatment before evaluation
options.explanation: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Order advanced imaging immediately
options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.
correct.deeperWhy: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in osteomyelitis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in osteomyelitis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

A.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
A.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

D.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of osteomyelitis. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically
options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

options.text: Rely primarily on pattern recognition
options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.
correct.deeperWhy: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
correct.clinicalPearl: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

B.guidance: Consider why Consider the differential diagnosis systematically is the key concept.

D.misconception: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing osteomyelitis, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.

mcqs.stem: A patient with HIV has a CD4 count of 75 cells/μL and presents with subacute headache, fever, and confusion. India ink stain of CSF is positive. What is the recommended initial treatment?

options.text: IV ceftriaxone + vancomycin for bacterial meningitis coverage
options.explanation: The India ink positivity confirms cryptococcal meningitis. Bacterial meningitis coverage is not indicated when the organism is identified. Antifungal therapy is required.

options.text: IV amphotericin B deoxycholate + flucytosine for 2 weeks (induction), then fluconazole
options.explanation: Positive India ink indicates Cryptococcus neoformans meningitis. Per IDSA/WHO guidelines, induction therapy is amphotericin B + flucytosine × 2 weeks, followed by consolidation with fluconazole 400mg/day × 8 weeks, then maintenance fluconazole 200mg/day. Do NOT start ART immediately — wait 4-6 weeks to avoid immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS).

options.text: Start ART immediately along with amphotericin B
options.explanation: Starting ART simultaneously with antifungal induction significantly increases risk of IRIS, which has high mortality in cryptococcal meningitis. ART should be deferred 4-6 weeks per guidelines.

options.text: IV fluconazole 800mg/day as monotherapy
options.explanation: Fluconazole monotherapy for induction has significantly higher mortality than amphotericin-based regimens. It is used for consolidation and maintenance, not induction.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! IV amphotericin B deoxycholate + flucytosine for 2 weeks (induction), then fluconazole.

sections.title: Clinical Approach
sections.content: Systematic approach to Osteomyelitis including recognition, diagnosis, and management.

imaging_reasoning.order_reasoning: MRI is most sensitive/specific; XR shows changes only after 10-14 days; bone scan if MRI contraindicated
imaging_reasoning.expected_findings: T1 low signal (edema replacing marrow), T2/STIR high signal, enhancement, periosteal reaction
imaging_reasoning.management_impact: Guides biopsy location, surgical planning, duration of antibiotics (6 weeks IV typical)
